repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.abstract: Includes both sides of Dorothy's correspondence with Willard D. Straight; letters from friends and relatives. Also materials on civic activities, correspondence on Willard Straight Hall, and correspondence on The New Republic and Asia magazines.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu

relatedmaterial.head: NOTES
relatedmaterial.p: Related collection:Dorothy Whitney Straight. Papers from Dartington Hall, #3782.

arrangement.head: SERIES LIST

p.ref: Willard D. Straight to Dorothy Whitney Straight
Congratulations on Whitney W. Straight's birth, 1912
Transcripts of Correspondence (not microfilmed or indexed)

arrangement.p: Series II. Other papers through 1911 Boxes 5-7, 9. Reels 11, 12
Series III. Other papers, 1912 through 1925 Boxes 8-10. Reels 12-14

p.ref: Willard D. Straight in France, 1917-1918 (see also #1260, reel 7)

arrangement.p: Series IV. The New Republic Boxes 11-12. Reels 14-16

odd.head: General
odd.p: Portions of this collection are available on microfilm. The guide to the microfilm is here.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Dorothy Whitney Straight Elmhirst papers, #3725. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Philanthropist, educator, publisher, patron of the arts and social activist. Dorothy Payne Whitney, daughter of William C. Whitney, a financier and secretary of the Navy under President Grover Cleveland, married Willard Dickerman Straight in 1911. Straight died in 1919, and she married Leonard Knight Elmhirst in 1925. Dorothy and Elmhirst purchased Dartington Hall, Devon, England, reconstructed the 14th century manor and founded a school on the property. Dartington Hall became a center for the arts and a leading coeducational progressive school. The New School for Social Research in New York was begun with the backing of the Whitney Estate in 1919. Dorothy was involved with the New York State Food Commission, the Womens Liberty Loan Committee, the Social Unit Plan, the Junior League, the War Camp Service Committee, the Womens Emergency Committee of the European Relief Council, the New School for Social Research, the Tuberculosis Preventorium for Children, the Pueblos Defense Committee, and the Mayors Committee of Women on National Defense. She supported individual students at the Bordentown Military Academy and Hobart College, and helped to found The New Republic.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Includes both sides of Dorothy's correspondence with Willard D. Straight, whom she married in 1911, dealing with their courtship, and with World War I while he was stationed in France, 1917-1918; letters from friends concerning social engagements and activities in New York State and Washington, D.C.; letters from her brothers, her sister-in-law Gertrude V. Whitney, and various nieces and nephews. Also notebooks from her school days, guardianship papers, newspaper clippings concerning her father's death, guest books and photographs documenting her early life; correspondence, newspaper clippings, and certificates documenting her civic activities; inventory (l volume) and miscellany concerning the house at 1130 Fifth Avenue; communications concerning Willard D. Straight's death and funeral arrangements in Paris, his will and photographs; correspondence about the building of Willard Straight Hall at Cornell University, and correspondence and statements concerning THE NEW REPUBLIC and ASIA magazines. Major correspondents include Beatrice Bend (later Mrs. Henry Prather Fletcher), Katharine L. Barney (later Mrs. Courtlandt D. Barnes), Maurice Casenave, Herbert and Louise Croly, James Curtis, Henry P. Davison, William A. Delano, Richard Derby, Margaret (Myra) G. Dix (later Mrs. Charles Lawrance), Martin Egan, Alfred W. Fiedler, Henry Prather Fletcher, Florence (Daisy) Jaffray Harriman, Louise (Tottie) R. Knowlton (later Mrs. Buell Hollister), and George D. Marvin.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Also, William and Caroline D. Phillips, Ethel C. Roosevelt (later Mrs. Richard Derby), Susan R. Sedgwick (widow of Arthur Swann, later Mrs. Paul L. Hammond), Hazel Straight (later Mrs. James Forest Sanborn), Eliza Morgan Swift, May Tuckerman (later Mrs. Hermann Kinnicutt), Pauline P. Whitney (later Mrs. Almeric H. Paget), Anna L.B. Williams, and Gladys M. Vanderbilt (later Countess Szechenyi), A. Piatt Andrew, J. Howland Auchincloss, Robert and Martha W. Bacon and their son Robert Low Bacon, Frances P.B. Bolton, William J. Calhoun, Wilbur R. Chenoweth, Winston Churchill, Ellen Straight Cross, Richard Harding Davis, Charles D. Draper, Lord Charles French, John Foord, Mary Harriman (later Mrs. Charles C. Rumsey), Herbert C. Hoover, Colonel Edward M. House, Delancey K. Jay, Walter Lippmann, Charles Merz, Ruth Morgan, Annah D. Ripley (later Countess de Viel Castel), Alice L. Roosevelt (later Mrs. Nicholas Longworth), Corinne Roosevelt (later Mrs. Douglas Robinson), Theodore Roosevelt, Elihu Root, Lillian Wald, Lloyd Warren, William Fitz Hugh (Sheldon) Whitehouse, and Stark Young.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Also one leather-bound writing set containing paper, pencil, and pen; marked in gold letters: "D. W. June 1910" (Dorothy Whitney).

accessrestrict.head: Access Restrictions:
accessrestrict.p: Access to the "Honeymoon scrapbook" in Box 9 restricted to the permission of the curator.

userestrict.head: Restrictions on Use:
userestrict.p: Edward C. Carter letter may not be reproduced.

did.unittitle: Willard D. Straight to Dorothy Whitney Straight
Nov. 28, 1910 - Dec. 1, 1924, Roosevelt family to DWS
June 12, 1909 - Jan. 1, 1922, Eliza M. Swift to DWS
June 22, 1904 - Aug. 8, 1905, May Tuckerman to DWS
May 16, 1906 - Dec. 2, 1907, May T. Kinnicutt to DWS
June 17, 1908 - 1922, May T. Kinnicutt to DWS
June 16, 1909 - Mar. 2, 1922, Lillian D. Wald to DWS
Mar. 3, 1911 - May 10, 1924, Stark Young to DWS
Congratulations on Whitney W. Straight's birth, 1912
Transcripts of Correspondence (not microfilmed or indexed)
Reprint, "A Gallant Lady"; correspondence concerning Flora P. Whitney biography - Mark Hirsh
Photos and newspaper clippings concerning W. C. Whitney, Old Westbury, Long Island (pre-1904)
Newspaper clippings concerning W. C. Whitney's death
Account of Harry P. Whitney, Dorothy's Testamentary Guardian,
Notes and clippings, Yale Junior Prom,
Aiken, South Carolina and W. C. Whitney's houses in Westbury, Long Island and New York City
Photos of house of Mr. and Mrs. Henry B. Payne, parents of Flora P. Whitney, Dorothy's mother
Home of George Washington, Mount Vernon - photo
Dorothy's trip to Europe, 1904 - mounted photos
Photos of Dorothy and friends through
World War I menu with recipes
Photos 1912-1925 (including wedding photo of Dorothy Whitney to Leonard K. Elmhirst)
Photo, 1917 (Wedding, Beatrice Bend - Henry Prather Fletcher)
Willard D. Straight in France, 1917-1918 (see also #1260, reel 7)
Appointment as Major of the infantry,
Handwritten copies of cables sent and received by Dorothy during and after Willard's illness
Cable exchnage relating to Willard's illness and death (Nov. 25, 1918 - Dec. 1, 1918)
Correspondence and photos relating to "Suresnes American Cemetary and Memorial"
Financial records, 1920, 1921, 1925 (including documents relating to sale)
Photo album, Willard Straight Hall (being built)
